summary: Joel Spolsky argues that the widespread adoption of Java as the primary teaching language in university CS programs is producing graduates who lack fundamental programming abilities. He contends that pointers and recursion — traditionally taught through C and Scheme — serve as essential 'weed-out' mechanisms that identify students with the mental flexibility required for great programming. By removing these difficult concepts, JavaSchools produce more graduates but fewer truly capable programmers. Spolsky connects this to real-world consequences, noting that Google's invention of MapReduce came directly from functional programming knowledge that JavaSchool graduates would lack. He concludes that these dumbed-down curricula fail both industry and academia.
key_points: Pointers and recursion are the two concepts that most reliably separate great programmers from mediocre ones, and Java curricula effectively bypass both
The shift to all-Java curricula was driven by pressure to reduce dropout rates and increase department budgets, not by pedagogical improvement
Google's MapReduce — trivially obvious to anyone who understood functional programming — illustrates the real-world cost of not teaching these fundamentals
OOP is not a suitable substitute for pointers and recursion as a weed-out mechanism because struggling with OOP still produces working programs
JavaSchool graduates are harder to evaluate in interviews because employers can't distinguish between lack of education and lack of aptitude
money_quotes: Java is not, generally, a hard enough programming language that it can be used to discriminate between great programmers and mediocre programmers.
Pointers and recursion require a certain ability to reason, to think in abstractions, and, most importantly, to view a problem at several levels of abstraction simultaneously.
I have never met anyone who can do Scheme, Haskell, and C pointers who can't pick up Java in two days, and create better Java code than people with five years of experience in Java.
The very fact that Google invented MapReduce, and Microsoft didn't, says something about why Microsoft is still playing catch up trying to get basic search features to work.

When you struggle with an OOP problem, your program still works, it's just sort of hard to maintain. Allegedly. But when you struggle with pointers, your program produces the line Segmentation Fault and you have no idea what's going on.
A JavaSchool that won't teach C and won't teach Scheme is not really teaching computer science, either.

I can't understand why the professors on the curriculum committees at CS schools have allowed their programs to be dumbed down to the point where not only can't they produce working programmers, they can't even produce CS grad students who might get PhDs and compete for their jobs. Oh wait. Never mind. Maybe I do understand.
key_insight: By replacing C and Scheme with Java, CS programs eliminated the intellectual challenges that develop and identify the capacity for multi-level abstract reasoning — the very skill that separates great programmers from adequate ones.
